question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / SetterRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer using SetterRatifier where the maker set isRootRatified[maker][root] = true for a root containing offer A, and then the taker submits a different (leafIndex, proof) combination that maps to a different leaf in the same tree (offer B), given that SetterRatifier only checks isRootRatified and HashLib.isLeaf without verifying which specific offer is being taken, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct specific offer? Proof idea: build a 2-leaf tree, ratify the root, call take with offer B parameters and offer B's proof path, assert isLeaf correctly validates offer B's hash.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where the offer struct passed to take differs from the offer struct that was hashed into the Merkle tree (e.g., different offer.callback or offer.callbackData), given that Midnight.take passes the offer struct directly to IRatifier.isRatified which then calls HashLib.hashOffer on the received struct, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the exact offer parameters? Proof idea: sign a root for offer with callback = address(0), submit take with offer.callback = maliciousCallback, assert hashOffer produces a different hash and isLeaf fails.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that the EcrecoverRatifier domain separator is computed dynamically (not cached as an immutable) using block.chainid at call time, meaning on a chain where block.chainid changes mid-execution (theoretical), the domain separator could differ between the signing time and the verification time, violating the invariant that the domain separator must be stable for the lifetime of signed offers? Proof idea: verify that block.chainid is stable within a single EVM execution context and cannot be manipulated by the taker.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where offer.reduceOnly = true but the ratifier signature was created for an offer with offer.reduceOnly = false, given that hashOffer includes offer.reduceOnly and a mismatch would cause isLeaf to fail, and whether a taker can modify offer.reduceOnly in the take call to bypass the MakerCreditOrDebtIncreased check, violating the invariant that offer parameters must match the signed intent? Proof idea: sign offer with reduceOnly = false, submit take with reduceOnly = true (or vice versa), assert hashOffer mismatch causes InvalidProof.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where offer.tick is modified from the signed value, given that hashOffer includes offer.tick and a mismatch would cause isLeaf to fail, and whether the tick spacing check (offer.tick % tickSpacing == 0) combined with the ratifier check provides sufficient protection against price manipulation, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct price? Proof idea: sign offer at tick T, submit take with tick T + tickSpacing (a valid accessible tick), assert hashOffer mismatch causes InvalidProof.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator of maker A call setIsAuthorized(operatorB, true, makerA) to grant operatorB full control over makerA's Midnight state, and then operatorB calls cancelRoot(makerA, root) on EcrecoverRatifier (which also checks isAuthorized), effectively allowing a chain of delegated cancellations that the original maker did not intend, violating the invariant that authorization must only allow intended account delegation? Proof idea: chain setIsAuthorized calls through two hops, assert the second-hop operator can cancel roots on behalf of the original maker.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where offer.maxAssets and offer.maxUnits are both zero (no cap), given that Midnight.take checks atMostOneNonZero(offer.maxAssets, offer.maxUnits) which passes when both are zero, and the consumed tracking would use consumed[maker][group] += units with no upper bound check (ConsumedUnits requires newConsumed <= offer.maxUnits = 0 which would always fail), violating the invariant that offer fill amounts must be bounded? Proof idea: create offer with maxAssets = 0 and maxUnits = 0, call take with units > 0, assert ConsumedUnits reverts because newConsumed > 0 = maxUnits.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit a ratifierData where the abi.decode fails (malformed bytes), causing a revert that propagates through Midnight.take as a generic revert rather than RatifierFail, and whether this allows a griefing attacker to cause take to revert for legitimate takers by front-running with a malformed ratifierData submission, violating the invariant that low-cost DoS must not block legitimate takes? Proof idea: submit malformed ratifierData bytes to take, observe the revert type, and assess whether the revert is distinguishable from RatifierFail.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where the offer.group is shared across multiple markets (same maker, same group bytes32 value, different market parameters), and filling offers in one market reduces the group budget for offers in another market, given that consumed[maker][group] is global across all markets, violating the invariant that consumption group budget cannot be exceeded across offers and testing whether cross-market group sharing creates unintended cancellation? Proof idea: create two offers in different markets with the same group and combined maxUnits = X, fill both partially, assert total consumed[maker][group] does not exceed X.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash reverts with TreeTooHigh for proof.length > 20, causing a revert in isRatified that propagates as a non-RatifierFail error through Midnight.take, and whether a griefing attacker can submit a proof array of length 21 to cause an unexpected revert type, violating the invariant that ratifier failures must be handled gracefully? Proof idea: submit ratifierData with proof.length = 21, observe that TreeTooHigh reverts inside isRatified, and check if Midnight.take catches this as RatifierFail or propagates the raw revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where the maker has authorized the EcrecoverRatifier contract (isAuthorized[maker][ecrecoverRatifier] == true) but the signature in ratifierData was created by a third party who is also authorized (isAuthorized[maker][thirdParty] == true), and whether the EcrecoverRatifier correctly validates that the recovered signer is either the maker or an authorized address at take time, violating the invariant that only the maker or their current authorized delegates can ratify offers? Proof idea: authorize both EcrecoverRatifier and thirdParty, have thirdParty sign a root, submit take with that signature, assert isRatified succeeds because thirdParty is authorized.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that the EcrecoverRatifier struct hash is keccak256(abi.encode(offerTreeTypeHash(proof.length), root)) where root is a bytes32, and whether a taker can find a root value that collides with a different OfferTree struct encoding (e.g., a tree of different height with the same root), given that the typehash changes with proof.length, violating the invariant that signatures must unambiguously bind to a single tree structure? Proof idea: for two different proof lengths N and M, check if any root value R satisfies both keccak256(abi.encode(typeHash_N, R)) and keccak256(abi.encode(typeHash_M, R)) being signable by the same key for different intents.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where offer.maker == address(0) (zero address maker), given that Midnight.take checks offer.maker != taker (SelfTake) but does not check offer.maker != address(0), and EcrecoverRatifier checks _signer == offer.maker which would require ecrecover to return address(0) (blocked by InvalidSignature), violating the invariant that offers must have a valid non-zero maker? Proof idea: construct offer with maker = address(0), call take, trace through all checks to identify which one first reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: Midnight.take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where the offer.ratifier is the EcrecoverRatifier but the maker has not authorized it (isAuthorized[maker][ecrecoverRatifier] == false), causing Midnight.take to revert with RatifierUnauthorized before even calling isRatified, and whether this check order (authorization before ratification) creates any TOCTOU vulnerability if authorization state changes between the check and the call? Proof idea: check authorization, call take, assert RatifierUnauthorized reverts; then authorize ratifier in a callback and check if reentrancy could bypass the authorization check.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker take an offer where the offer.expiry is in the past relative to the signing time but in the future relative to the take time (i.e., the maker signed an offer with a far-future expiry, and the taker waits until just before expiry), and whether the absence of a per-offer nonce means the maker cannot invalidate the offer without canceling the entire Merkle root, violating the invariant that makers must be able to cancel individual offers without affecting other offers in the same tree? Proof idea: sign a tree with two offers A and B, cancel root to invalidate A, assert B is also invalidated, quantifying the collateral damage of root-level cancellation.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that the EcrecoverRatifier does not validate that offer.ratifier == address(this) (i.e., the ratifier field in the offer does not need to match the EcrecoverRatifier being called), meaning a maker could sign an offer with ratifier = setterRatifier but the taker submits it to EcrecoverRatifier (if authorized), and whether this creates cross-ratifier confusion, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct ratifier contract? Proof idea: sign offer with ratifier = setterRatifier, authorize ecrecoverRatifier, submit take with offer.ratifier = ecrecoverRatifier but ratifierData for ecrecoverRatifier, assert hashOffer includes ratifier field so the proof fails.
